feedback.text: Good, I would like to add a few things to the proposal. Currently, when you sell a game on any page, what you get is a ‘licence’, which allows companies to take off access to them for any reason, without any compensation or explanation. These companies should have the right to block access to malicious users by breaking the rules of the game, such as cheats, malware, language abuse, etc. But they should not have the right to remove the product for which you have paid, simply because you have left a new one or do not want to keep it. At the very cost of creating the game, an end-of-service plan should be added, with an implementation such as setting up a hosty system for a multi-player game, or removing the ‘always online’ mode from the games by a juegador, something that even the developers of these companies say is very easy to implement. Or at least not to close community projects, created so that buyers of these products can continue to play their part, even when companies stop supporting them, an example of this is ‘Plutonium’, this community project allows anyone who has the original products purchased to keep playing it safely, which the company does not do. I remain with this example if you want to play an old call of duty, there is currently no way of playing it safely without a community project, in many cases when you enter these products you are sure that a malicious actor will have access to your computer. These companies NOT keep their products, which most of them continue to sell at full outlet price. If these companies are not going to maintain their products, they should not have the right to close community projects that REQUIEREN the purchase of the original product, created to be able to play them safely. One of the defences of these companies against these implementations or projects is security, which is the most hypocritic excuse in the world, since they do not maintain the safety and functioning of their earlier products, which are mostly sold at full price, for a suboptima experience, in which you do not have access to most of the game, either because of security or because they have closed down multi-player servers. This is as if you were sold a pellet and when the next one, the company loses it, makes it impossible to see it completely, if you are no longer able to maintain the security that you see if there is a malicious actor who can access all your information (bank details, photos, passwords, accounts, etc.). As I stated above if the company: 1oFor multi-player games: It gives a hostee system of servers, or leaves buyers to make a community project in order to remain Jewish, they would not have to maintain the products, which would make companies profit by selling the original product, without having to do any kind of maintenance. 2° for games a player: Remove ‘always online’ from a completely unnecessary requirement in this type of games, except to prevent them from playing once the company closes the servers.
